column2: Despite some conflicts, both converged towards post-war social transformation, aided by the USSR and local communist movements.

column1: In which non-European countries did anti-fascism and national liberation coincide, aiding communist triumph?
column2: China, Korea, and Indochina (Vietnam, Cambodia, Laos) where Japan was the colonial power.

column1: How did leaders of decolonizing states outside Asia typically relate to communism during 1941-45?
column2: They were mostly Left-leaning but less constrained by anti-Axis priority and pursued national liberation independently.

column1: What post-war hopes did colonial leaders have regarding the two superpowers?
column2: Optimism, as both the USA and USSR officially opposed old colonialism and weakened its legitimacy.

column1: Did colonial empires resist decolonization peacefully?
column2: No, they fought savage rearguard actions to maintain control despite global changes.

column1: Which countries’ populations fought most stubbornly for the Axis cause?
column2: Germany and Japan fought with loyalty and efficiency until the very end.

column1: Outside its core countries, where did fascism find support during WWII?
column2: Mostly ideological minorities on the radical right, nationalist groups allied to Germany, and auxiliary forces recruited during occupation.

column1: What was the main appeal of European fascism to conservatives?
column2: Fascism was seen as a safeguard against socialism, communism, and the Soviet Union (“godless devil’s headquarters”).

column1: Why did fascism fail to survive politically after WWII?
column2: Defeat discredited it; it lacked universal appeal and offered nothing to post-war societies except memories of defeat.

column1: Which country maintained a modest neo-fascist political presence post-war?
column2: Italy, with the Movimento Sociale Italiano honoring Mussolini.

column1: How did post-war German and Japanese societies react to their fascist pasts?
column2: Many found old beliefs counterproductive; they had to adjust to new political and social systems imposed by occupying powers.

column1: How did Austrian politics evolve after WWII?
column2: Quickly reverted to pre-1933 democratic politics, with a slight shift to the Left.

column1: What was the ideological foundation of the anti-fascist alliance?
column2: Enlightenment values: reason, science, education, popular government, equality, and progress.

column1: Did any non-democratic states adopt “Democratic” in their official names post-war?
column2: Yes, states like Ethiopia, Somalia (pre-Siad Barre), North Korea, Algeria, and East Germany used names like “Democratic Republic” despite authoritarianism.

column1: How did Western constitutional capitalism, communism, and third-world states compare on rights and secularism?
column2: All committed to racial and gender equality and secular governance, though all fell short in practice.

column1: What was the dominant economic policy approach globally after WWII until the 1970s?
column2: Active state management and planning of the economy, rejecting full market freedom.

column1: How did capitalist governments view economic interventionism post-WWII?
column2: As necessary to prevent a repeat of interwar economic crises and political radicalization.

column1: What economic ideology did third-world countries adopt after decolonization?
column2: Socialism inspired by the Soviet Union, emphasizing public action against backwardness and dependency.

column1: What was the Soviet Union’s economic planning approach?
column2: Central planning and state control over the economy.

column1: How did the defeat of the Axis powers influence expectations about social transformation?
column2: It created optimism that political mobilization and revolutionary policies would bring a new era of social transformation.

column1: Did the post-war social transformation proceed as planned by national leaders?
column2: No, history followed unpredictable paths; the first major challenge was the breakdown of the anti-fascist alliance.

column1: What happened to the alliance between capitalism and communism after WWII?
column2: With no fascism to unite against, capitalism and communism became mortal enemies again, leading to the Cold War.

column1: What did Walter Benjamin describe as the "lyric poetry of Surrealism"?
column2: The surrealists’ Paris as a small "universe" full of ghostly signals, inconceivable analogies, and connections, reflecting the cosmos.

column1: What was H.L. Mencken’s opinion on the progress of the New Architecture in the USA in 1931?
column2: He believed it made little progress, mostly confined to factory design, with advocates being earnest but not widely convincing.

column1: By 1914, which major avant-garde movements or styles were already established under ‘modernism’?
column2: Cubism, expressionism, futurism, pure abstraction in painting, functionalism in architecture, abandonment of tonality in music, and literary breaks with tradition.

column1: Which generation dominated modernism by 1914 and even 40 years later?
column2: The generation born by the 1880s, including figures like T.S. Eliot, Alban Berg, and Anton Webern.

column1: What were the two main formal avant-garde innovations after 1914?
column2: Dadaism (Western Europe) and Constructivism (Soviet East).

column1: What was Constructivism and how did it influence art and architecture?
column2: An avant-garde movement focused on skeletal, mechanical constructions; it influenced architecture and industrial design through the Bauhaus but had few realized monumental projects.

column1: How did Dadaism originate and what was its artistic stance?
column2: Born among exiles in Zurich in 1916 as a nihilist protest against WWI and bourgeois society, it rejected traditional art and embraced scandal and randomness.

column1: What was Marcel Duchamp’s famous contribution to Dada art?
column2: Exhibiting a public urinal as ‘readymade art’ in 1917, challenging traditional concepts of art.

column1: How did Surrealism differ from Dadaism?
column2: Surrealism combined rejection of traditional art with a positive plea for reviving imagination through the unconscious, psychoanalysis, magic, and dreams.

column1: Which countries were the main centers of Surrealism?
column2: France primarily, and countries influenced by France such as Spain and Hispanic America.

column1: Name some key surrealist artists and their contribution to culture.
column2: Max Ernst, René Magritte, Joan Miró, Salvador Dalí—famous for bizarre, dreamlike images influencing poetry, painting, and cinema.

column1: How did Surrealism influence cinema and photography?
column2: Inspired filmmakers like Luis Buñuel and Jacques Prévert; influenced photojournalist Henri Cartier-Bresson’s style.

column1: What was the relationship between avant-garde art and mass public taste from 1914–1945?
column2: Avant-garde art became more central to established culture but remained largely isolated from the tastes of the mass public.

column1: How did avant-garde art supplement traditional forms like opera during this period?
column2: It supplemented rather than replaced classics, proving cultural seriousness, while ballet was transformed into an avant-garde medium.

column1: Who was Sergei Diaghilev and what was his contribution to avant-garde ballet?
column2: A Russian impresario who modernized ballet by integrating avant-garde music, choreography, and designs from artists like Picasso and Braque.

column1: What was the significance of the 1917 ballet Parade?
column2: It was a landmark modernist ballet combining cubist designs, music by Satie, and libretto by Jean Cocteau, marking avant-garde’s breakthrough into mainstream culture.

column1: How did the public’s attitude toward modernism change after WWI?
column2: The philistine opposition quieted, and the avant-garde gained snob appeal, vogue, and elite artistic status, making modernism more accepted.

column1: What was Diaghilev’s ballet an example of?
column2: A medium for diffusing avant-garde arts, though its success varied by country.

column1: Which cities formed the competing avant-garde cultural axis in Europe?
column2: Moscow and Berlin, which rivaled Paris until Stalin and Hitler suppressed them.

column1: Why were some European literatures isolated from international influence?
column2: Due to language barriers and lack of translations until the 1930s anti-fascist diaspora.

column1: When did Spanish-language poetry gain international recognition?
column2: During the Spanish Civil War (1936–39).

column1: Which two avant-garde arts were widely admired across all countries?
column2: Film and jazz.

column1: Who was the most universally admired film personality by the avant-garde?
column2: Charlie Chaplin.

column1: Which countries saw avant-garde artists dominate film production?
column2: Weimar Germany and Soviet Russia.

column1: What film by Eisenstein became a global avant-garde classic?
column2: Battleship Potemkin (1925), especially the Odessa Steps sequence.

column1: Which style of cinema did intellectuals favor from the mid-1930s?
column2: Populist French cinema – Renoir, Clair, Carné.

column1: How did Hollywood integrate avant-garde elements?
column2: By hiring European directors and imitating German expressionist models.

column1: Why did the avant-garde admire jazz?
column2: Jazz symbolized modernity and cultural revolution.

column1: What symbol of modernism was popular at the Bauhaus?
column2: A saxophone, appearing in staff photos.

column1: Which English authors were considered ‘OK names’ among literary schoolboys?
column2: T.S. Eliot, Ezra Pound, James Joyce, and D.H. Lawrence.

column1: How did avant-garde critics like F.R. Leavis revise literary canons?
column2: They rejected Dickens and elevated authors like John Donne.

column1: Which Spanish painter fell out of favor, and which became admired?
column2: Murillo was rejected; El Greco gained admiration.

column1: How did modernism affect commercial design?
column2: Influenced industrial design, advertising, and everyday objects.

column1: What chair by Breuer became a symbol of modern design?
column2: The tubular steel stacking chair (1925–29).

column1: How did Art Deco and streamlining reflect modernist ideals?
column2: By adapting abstraction and futurism for mass consumer appeal.

column1: What was the Bauhaus?
column2: A German art/design school (1919–1933) shaping modern architecture and design.

column1: What was the Bauhaus’s shift in design philosophy after 1921?
column2: From fine arts to practical industrial design (cars, aircraft seats, graphics).

column1: Why was the Bauhaus considered subversive?
column2: Because of its radical, left-leaning artistic approach and ideas.

column1: What was a major trend in 1930s serious arts?
column2: Political commitment, mainly to the Left, but also to reactionary ideologies.

column1: Which famous authors held reactionary or fascist sympathies?
column2: T.S. Eliot, Ezra Pound, Knut Hamsun, Céline, Yeats.

column1: How did Russian émigré artists view Bolshevism?
column2: They generally rejected it, though they had varied political leanings.

column1: Which political side primarily attracted the avant-garde in the 1930s and 1940s?
column2: The revolutionary Left, especially during the anti-fascist era.

column1: What did Lenin's influence do for Marxism and the avant-garde?
column2: He revived Marxism as a revolutionary ideology in the West, helping convert many avant-garde movements.

column1: What term did the Nazis use to label leftist avant-garde art?
column2: "Cultural Bolshevism" (Kulturbolschewismus).

column1: Which movements followed Dada in supporting revolution?
column2: Surrealism, with many followers choosing Trotsky over Stalin.

column1: What symbolized the Moscow–Berlin cultural connection in the Weimar Republic?
column2: Shared political sympathies and cooperation, e.g., Mies van der Rohe's monument for Communist leaders.

column1: Why did Western architects work in the USSR during the Great Slump?
column2: The USSR was ideologically appealing and offered professional opportunities during Western economic crisis.

column1: How did even apolitical cinema reflect radicalism in Weimar Germany?
column2: Through directors like G.W. Pabst, who produced radical films before working under the Nazis.

column1: What tragedy befell modernist artists during the rise of authoritarianism?
column2: Their political movements rejected them in favor of traditional, monumental styles.

column1: Which fascist regime showed some support for Futurism?
column2: Italian Fascism, though partially.

column1: What architectural style did authoritarian regimes favor?
column2: Monumental, classical forms with ideological clarity.

column1: What happened to avant-garde art in Germany and Russia under Hitler and Stalin?
column2: It was suppressed; both countries vanished from the avant-garde cultural scene.

column1: Who was Albert Speer?
column2: Hitler's chosen architect who realized his monumental artistic visions.

column1: What was Anatol Lunacharsky’s role in Soviet culture?
column2: As Commissar for Enlightenment, he supported avant-garde artists despite Lenin's conservative tastes.

column1: How did Prussian social-democrats support avant-garde music?
column2: They empowered conductor Otto Klemperer to transform Berlin opera into a center for new music.

column1: What did turbulent times bring to artistic expression in Central and Eastern Europe?
column2: Heightened emotion and tragic vision, often giving voice to even modest talents.

column1: Who was B. Traven?
column2: An obscure anarchist who gained recognition through moving stories, e.g., The Treasure of the Sierra Madre.

column1: What happened to George Grosz's art after emigrating to the U.S.?
column2: He lost his political edge and became sentimental.

column1: What was characteristic of Central European avant-garde during the "Age of Cataclysm"?
column2: A tragic, harsh vision—art from apocalypse, rarely hopeful.

column1: Examples of Central European tragic avant-garde works?
column2: Berg’s Wozzeck, Brecht-Weill’s Threepenny Opera, Babel’s Red Cavalry, Döblin’s Berlin Alexanderplatz.

column1: What literary works arose from the collapse of the Habsburg Empire?
column2: Kraus’s The Last Days of Humanity, Hašek’s The Good Soldier Schwejk, Roth’s Radetzkymarsch.

column1: Which Austrian poet captured the end-of-order mood?
column2: Rainer Maria Rilke, in Duino Elegies.

column1: What was Franz Kafka’s contribution?
column2: A deep expression of existential and societal incomprehension.

column1: Who was Walter Benjamin’s "Angel of History"?
column2: A metaphor from Paul Klee’s painting, representing history as endless catastrophe driven by the storm of progress.

column1: How did Western intellectuals’ mood differ from Central Europeans'?
column2: Less desperate; they still believed in defending and revitalizing society.

column1: What did many Western thinkers believe about the USSR?
column2: That it embodied Enlightenment values and "progress" despite Stalinist repression.

column1: How did ultra-reactionaries like Waugh and Céline view the world?
column2: As black comedy or a nightmare, unlike hopeful Western reformists.

column1: Who were major British avant-garde figures?
column2: Poet W.H. Auden, sculptor Henry Moore, composer Benjamin Britten.

column1: Where was the avant-garde concept primarily confined?
column2: Europe and its cultural outposts, not yet in the global South or even New York.

column1: What defined non-European artistic concerns in this period?
column2: Modernity, not modernism—how to adapt old languages and traditions to modern needs.

column1: What artistic movement dominated in the non-Western world?
column2: Realism, often tied to revolutionary politics and social critique.

column1: Who was Lu Hsün and what did he advocate?
column2: First great modern Chinese writer; admired Russian literature for its portrayal of the oppressed.

column1: What challenge faced non-Western artists regarding language?
column2: Creating literary forms from vernacular languages, e.g., Urdu, Turkish, Bengali.

column1: What two tools made the life of common people more visible in the 20th century?
column2: Reportage and the camera, both entered a "golden age" after 1914, enabling unprecedented documentation of ordinary lives.

column1: Which American writers were also journalists or newspapermen?
column2: Ernest Hemingway, Theodore Dreiser, and Sinclair Lewis – they combined journalism with literature.

column1: What genre became accepted under Russian avant-garde influence?
column2: Reportage, first appearing in dictionaries in the late 1920s and seen as socially critical literature and art.

column1: Who popularized reportage in Central Europe?
column2: Egon Erwin Kisch, known as the “Reporter in a Rush,” whose 1925 book helped spread the term.

column1: Which novel used avant-garde documentary techniques like "Camera Eye"?
column2: John Dos Passos’ trilogy USA – used experimental techniques inspired by Dziga Vertov.

column1: What did “March of Time” documentaries represent?
column2: A blend of journalism and avant-garde techniques to elevate movie newsreels into high-status documentary forms.

column1: Which magazines symbolized the golden age of photojournalism?
column2: Life (USA), Picture Post (UK), Vu (France) – all inspired by communist photography innovations.

column1: What helped make candid photography easier after 1924?
column2: The Leica camera, a miniature device that allowed unposed, spontaneous shots.

column1: Why did people believe in photography’s objectivity?
column2: “The camera cannot lie” – a belief that photos represented real truth.

column1: What medium dominated public attention in the 1930s?
column2: Cinema, with ticket sales often outpacing newspaper sales, especially in Britain.

column1: What characterized the golden ages of film in certain countries?
column2: A fusion of avant-garde and mass appeal in Germany (silent), France (sound), and Italy (post-fascism).

column1: Why was 1930s French cinema special among highbrow films?
column2: It balanced intellectual culture with mass entertainment, focusing on stories, crime, love, and humor.

column1: Why did avant-garde art often fail to reach wider audiences?
column2: Because its political/artistic radicalism appealed only to small minorities.

column1: What shift in cultural dominance occurred in the early 20th century?
column2: Mass arts overtook traditional middle-class arts, becoming culturally hegemonic.

column1: Which literary genre exploded in popularity in the interwar years?
column2: Detective fiction, mainly British, with figures like Agatha Christie leading the genre.

column1: What was the typical setting of a classic detective story?
column2: A stable, ordered environment (like a country house), disrupted by murder, with order restored through rational detection.

column1: Why is classic detective fiction seen as conservative?
column2: It restored order and affirmed social norms, often using a detective representing the upper/middle class.

column1: What genre emerged as a more paranoid counterpart to detective stories?
column2: The secret agent thriller, often British, more hysterical and reflecting a world under threat.

column1: How did newspaper circulation in the USA change from 1920–1950?
column2: It doubled, outpacing population growth and contributing to the mass media boom.

column1: What was unique about British newspaper consumption?
column2: The British bought 600 papers per 1,000 people, an exceptionally high rate.

column1: How did newspapers appeal to less literate readers?
column2: By using pictures, comic strips, and simple, eye-catching language.

column1: What was the impact of talking films on literacy?
column2: Reduced the need for literacy, especially for English-speaking audiences after the late 1920s.

column1: What effect did sound film have on global culture?
column2: It helped spread spoken English, reinforcing its future as a global lingua franca.

column1: What was Hollywood's role in the global film industry by 1937?
column2: Hegemonic – it produced as many films as the rest of the world combined.

column1: What was the difference between US and USSR film production in the 1930s?
column2: In 1937 the USA produced 567 films, the USSR only 41, showing capitalist dominance.

column1: What new medium transformed global communication in the 20th century?
column2: Radio, a powerful, intimate mass communication tool.

column1: What limited early radio access to developed countries?
column2: The cost of radio sets, making them initially accessible only to wealthier populations.

column1: What was special about radio adoption in Britain by 1939?
column2: Half of the 9 million sets were owned by people with modest incomes, showing wide accessibility.

column1: How did radio change the lives of poor, housebound people?
column2: It brought the world into their homes, breaking loneliness and offering constant information and entertainment.

column1: How many US households owned radios by 1939?
column2: Over 27 million, showing the explosive growth of the medium.

column1: Why did radio not change perception like film did?
column2: It was a medium, not a message – it transmitted, but didn’t reshape sensory experiences.

column1: What made radio powerful despite not changing perception?
column2: It could speak to millions at once, yet feel personal – ideal for propaganda and ads.

column1: Which world leaders used radio effectively in the 1930s?
column2: FDR (USA) with his Fireside Chats and King George V (UK) with royal Christmas broadcasts.

column1: When did radio usage in Europe surge significantly?
column2: During and after the Second World War, except in countries devastated by battle.

column1: Why did governments resist commercial radio in many countries?
column2: Because they wanted to retain control over such a powerful tool for shaping public opinion.

column1: What model of broadcasting did the UK maintain?
column2: Public monopoly via the BBC, avoiding commercial influence.

column1: What everyday formats did radio pioneer?
column2: Sports commentary, news bulletins, celebrity guest shows, soap operas, and serial programs.

column1: What major change did radio bring to daily life?
column2: It privatized and structured time, affecting both work and leisure with fixed schedules.

column1: How did radio create a new public sphere?
column2: People shared experiences by hearing the same programs or speeches, creating a collective culture.

column1: Which art was most affected by radio?
column2: Music – it removed acoustic limitations and allowed mass, continuous listening.

column1: What medium brought music before radio?
column2: The gramophone, although not yet widely accessible before WWI.

column1: How did radio expand music’s reach?
column2: Broadcasts could reach limitless audiences and play music longer than records.

column1: Did radio change the nature of music?
column2: No – it changed music’s role in life, not its structure or content.

column1: What forces dominated the popular arts?
column2: Technological and industrial: press, camera, film, record, radio.

column1: Where did creative innovation emerge?
column2: In urban entertainment districts since the late 19th century.

column1: When did tango peak and who was its star?
column2: In the 1920s–30s; Carlos Gardel.

column1: What genre symbolized Brazil like tango did Argentina?
column2: Samba – born from Rio Carnival’s democratization in the 1920s.

column1: What was jazz’s origin and impact?
column2: Emerged in U.S. cities from Black migration; became globally influential.

column1: How did blues evolve globally?
column2: It turned into rock-and-roll, a global youth idiom by mid-20th century.

column1: Which country dominated early global pop culture?
column2: The United States, due to economic power and mass media.

column1: Did other regions have global pop culture reach?
column2: Rarely – some regional influences like Egyptian music existed.

column1: What was the only cultural sphere not U.S.-dominated?
column2: Sport – especially association football.

column1: How did football go global?
column2: It spread with British economic influence and needed little equipment.

column1: What was the status of athletes before TV era?
column2: Mostly amateurs or modestly paid professionals.

column1: What limited early sports broadcasting?
column2: Radio could only convey events through audio, lacking visuals.

column1: Were major music stage works part of opera houses?
column2: No – most post-1918 masterpieces like Porgy and Bess stood apart.

column1: Who was Masterda inspired by in the struggle for Indian independence?
column2: Dan Breen, whose book My Fight for Irish Freedom influenced Masterda, leading him to model the Indian Republican Army (Chittagong branch) after the Irish Republican Army.

column1: Why did colonial administrators tolerate corruption, according to M. Carritt?
column2: Because it was a cheap method of controlling dissident populations by enabling people to gain favors in exchange for loyalty, hospitality, or support rather than just money.

column1: What model did non-Western elites often follow in the 20th century for development?
column2: They imitated Western or Soviet models, both emphasizing modernization and industrialization, viewing them as the only viable paths to progress.

column1: What was the primary method through which Western nations established superiority in the 19th century?
column2: By means of their economic and social systems, organization, and technology, not necessarily by direct occupation.

column1: What did E.P. Thompson criticize about Western attitudes towards the non-Western world?
column2: He criticized the "enormous condescension" and ethnocentric superiority often shown by Western societies.

column1: What was the effect of religious or traditional beliefs when opposing development, according to the text?
column2: They generally led to failure and defeat if they interfered with technological and economic development.

column1: What was the main limitation of traditional or neo-traditional ideologies in liberation movements before the 1970s?
column2: They failed to inspire or achieve successful mass mobilizations for liberation, with most successes driven by Western ideologies like nationalism or socialism.

column1: How did Western-educated elites influence the history of Third World countries?
column2: Despite being numerically small, they played a crucial role in shaping modernization efforts, leading political movements, and adopting Western political forms.

column1: Why was Soviet communism attractive to newly independent states?
column2: Because it offered a model of industrialization and overcoming backwardness through planning, not just proletarian revolution.

column1: What unifying sentiment could bridge elites and masses in colonial societies?
column2: Resentment against white racism, which could be shared across different social strata.

column1: How did capitalism in the Age of Empire affect global economies?
column2: It penetrated and transformed nearly all parts of the globe by integrating countries into the world market mainly as suppliers of raw materials and consumers of northern manufactured goods.

column1: What role did British overseas investment play before World War I?
column2: Over three quarters of British overseas investment was in government stocks, railways, ports, and shipping, fueling infrastructure to exploit dependent countries’ resources.

column1: Why was industrialization generally not part of the plan for dependent countries?
column2: Northern governments and entrepreneurs expected dependent countries to pay for imported manufactured goods by selling primary products, keeping them as suppliers rather than industrial producers.

column1: What happened to the British cotton industry after World War I?
column2: Local industries in India and China began producing cotton goods domestically, reducing reliance on expensive British imports and hurting the Lancashire cotton industry.

column1: How much of the world’s industrial output was produced by Western Europe and North America as late as 1960?
column2: Over 70% of gross world output and nearly 80% of the world’s manufacturing value added were produced in these regions.

column1: When did the significant shift of industrial production away from the West begin?
column2: In the last third of the 20th century, particularly from the 1970s onward, with the rise of the new international division of labor and deindustrialization of old heartlands.

column1: What was one reason the old industrial powers kept industry concentrated in their homelands?
column2: Protectionist policies and the desire to prevent emerging competitors from harming their domestic industries.

column1: Which colonial power systematically industrialized its colonies before 1945?
column2: Japan, which developed heavy industries in Korea, Manchuria, and Taiwan to serve its national industrialization needs.

column1: How did the Great Depression of 1929–33 affect industrialization in colonies?
column2: Financial pressures forced colonial governments to increase duties on manufactured goods and encouraged Western firms to start local production in dependent markets.

column1: What was the general state of the dependent world’s economy in the first half of the 20th century?
column2: It remained overwhelmingly agrarian and rural, with limited industrial development.

column1: How were most dependent regions controlled despite some native authorities remaining?
column2: Foreign powers exercised strong influence through advisors or extraterritorial rights, effectively dominating local governance.

column1: Why is the post-colonial world largely divided by imperial frontiers?
column2: Because imperial conquest and rivalry arbitrarily drew borders without regard for existing local social or political structures, creating states based on colonial boundaries rather than indigenous realities.

column1: What made the idea of a sovereign state difficult to grasp in many colonized regions?
column2: Many local peoples lived in intermingled communities without fixed territorial boundaries or centralized authority, making the Western concept of the sovereign state foreign and artificial.

column1: Why was it difficult to form a common front against imperialists in colonial countries?
column2: Because the traditionalist masses resented Western modernization, while the elites believed modernization was indispensable, creating a divide within the population.

column1: How did Bal Gangadhar Tilak gain support among Indians in early nationalism?
column2: By defending Hindu traditions like cow sanctity and child marriage, and asserting Hindu civilization's spiritual superiority over Western civilization.

column1: What was Gandhi's approach to mobilizing Indian masses?
column2: He combined nationalism with Hindu spirituality, promoted non-violent non-cooperation, and social reforms like rejecting the caste system, aiming to unite traditionalists and modernizers.

column1: Did Gandhi fully succeed in reconciling traditional mass sentiments with modernization?
column2: No, by the end of his life, Gandhi recognized the fundamental conflict between what moved the masses and what modernization required remained unresolved.

column1: What major political force in India represents the tradition of Tilak’s anti-modernism today?
column2: The militant BJP Party, which remains a divisive force in Indian politics and society.

column1: How did modernizers in the Muslim world try to promote reform?
column2: Through educational efforts by figures like Jamal al-Din al Afghani and Mohammed Abduh, focusing on schools and colleges rather than mass mobilization.

column1: Who were the real revolutionaries in the Islamic world in the 20th century?
column2: Secular modernizers like Kemal Atatürk, who broke traditional links between Islam, State, and Law.

column1: What form of mass mobilization was most effective in the Islamic world?
column2: Anti-modern mass piety, or Islamic fundamentalism, which appealed widely to the masses.

column1: How prominent were anti-imperialist movements before 1914?
column2: They were relatively limited, despite later rapid decolonization, with the British Empire facing the most serious colonial challenges.

column1: What changes occurred to colonial empires as a result of World War I?
column2: The German and Ottoman empires collapsed, and colonial unrest grew, leading to political concessions in colonies like Egypt and India.

column1: What was the significance of the Amritsar Massacre of 1919?
column2: It radicalized Indian nationalist politics by fueling mass resentment and strengthening calls for independence.

column1: How did Gandhi’s civil disobedience campaign affect British rule in India?
column2: It caused widespread unrest, making India intermittently ungovernable, but Gandhi’s moderation helped prevent total insurrection and preserve British control longer.

column1: What was the British ruling class’s view on Indian self-rule after 1919?
column2: They largely accepted that some form of Indian self-rule or dominion status was inevitable and began negotiating with Indian elites.

column1: Why did Britain eventually not strongly resist decolonization after WWII?
column2: Because the British Empire was weakened and its position less secure after two world wars, unlike other empires like France and the Netherlands, which resisted more.

column1: What was the major colonial problem France faced after WWI?
column2: The unfinished conquest of Morocco and the rebellion led by Abd-el-Krim in the Rif Mountains, which was defeated by 1926.

column1: When did modern anti-colonial movements develop in French colonies?
column2: Mainly after World War I, with earlier efforts limited to small movements like those in Tunisia.

column1: What impact did the Great Slump of 1929-33 have on the dependent world?
column2: It shook the entire dependent world, causing economic collapse, political destabilization, and increased tensions between dependent and metropolitan economies.

column1: How did imperialism affect ordinary people in export-oriented regions before the Slump?
column2: It brought substantial changes to their lives, including transformations in agriculture, labor migration, and new political awareness even in remote communities.

column1: How did the rise of rubber cultivation in Negri Sembilan (Malaysia) affect local inheritance customs?
column2: It shifted inheritance patterns from matrilineal to more patrilineal, empowering patriarchal Islamic leaders and altering social structures.

column1: What was the impact of growing port cities like Bombay and Shanghai on the dependent world?
column2: These cities became vital industrial and communication hubs linking the dependent world to the global economy, experiencing rapid population growth and labor conflict.

column1: Why did the Great Slump cause conflict between dependent and metropolitan economies?
column2: Because primary product prices fell more dramatically than manufactured goods, harming dependent economies reliant on exports.

column1: What was a key result of the Great Slump for colonial populations?
column2: It created a mass basis for political mobilization, especially among peasants involved in cash-crop economies, leading to increased unrest and demands for change.

column1: How did the 1930s political landscape in the Third World begin to change?
column2: Politicized minorities connected with common people, leading to mass politics such as Indian Congress's rise, Latin American populism, and communist resistance in Vietnam.

column1: What was the political situation in French North Africa in the 1930s?
column2: Serious political movements emerged for the first time in Tunisia and Algeria, with mass agitation growing in French Indochina under communist leadership.

column1: How did the Dutch maintain control over Indonesia despite opposition forces?
column2: The opposition—Islamic, communist, and secular nationalist—were divided and often fought among themselves, preventing unified resistance.

column1: What was the state of political unrest in sub-Saharan Africa during the 1930s?
column2: It remained mostly quiet politically, with some labor strikes starting after 1935, and colonial governments only beginning to recognize the destabilizing effects of economic changes.

column1: Which African cities had flourishing political presses in the mid-1930s?
column2: The Gold Coast (African Morning Post), Nigeria (West African Pilot), and Ivory Coast (Éclaireur de la Côte d’Ivoire).

column1: Who were some future African nationalist leaders active in the 1930s?
column2: Jomo Kenyatta of Kenya and Dr. Namdi Azikiwe of Nigeria.

column1: Did colonial powers in 1939 believe the end of empires was imminent?
column2: No, even hopeful Marxists did not expect the universal end of colonial empires before the Second World War changed the situation.

column1: How did the Second World War affect colonial empires?
column2: It was an inter-imperialist war that weakened the great colonial empires; France collapsed, Japan overran many colonies, and British control was limited to Africa south of the desert.

column1: Which colonial empire was liquidated by the British during WWII?
column2: The Italian Empire in the Horn of Africa was liquidated by the British with little trouble.

column1: What fatally damaged the old colonial powers after WWII?
column2: The proof that white men and their states could be defeated and that the old colonial powers were too weak to restore their positions, even after victory.

column1: What was significant about the Indian soldiers' defection during WWII?
column2: Up to 55,000 Indian soldiers defected to form the Indian National Army under Subhas Chandra Bose, seeking Japanese support for Indian independence.

column1: How did Japan position itself in its occupied territories during WWII?
column2: Japan claimed to be a liberator of colonies based on skin-colour solidarity and organized the "Assembly of Greater East Asiatic Nations" to promote this idea.

column1: Which countries gained independence shortly after WWII?
column2: Syria and Lebanon (1945), India and Pakistan (1947), Burma, Ceylon (Sri Lanka), Palestine (Israel), and Indonesia (1948).

column1: How did the USA treat the Philippines after WWII?
column2: The USA granted formal independence to the Philippines in 1946, ending its occupation since 1898.

column1: What was the outcome of French efforts to retain control in Indochina?
column2: The French were defeated in 1954 by communist resistance led by Ho Chi Minh and forced to withdraw, though the USA then maintained a divided Vietnam.

column1: Why did the Dutch abandon their Indonesian colony?
column2: The USA did not see Indonesia as strategically important against communism, especially after Indonesian nationalists suppressed a local communist uprising in 1948.

column1: What challenges did the British face in Malaya after WWII?
column2: They fought a primarily Chinese communist guerrilla insurgency for 12 years, requiring extensive military and police resources before decolonization in 1957.

column1: How did Britain approach Indian independence compared to the French and Dutch decolonization?
column2: Britain peacefully withdrew from India and neighboring countries without major resistance, recognizing nationalist movements and conceding power.

column1: What tragic event accompanied the partition of India in 1947?
column2: The partition led to religious massacres killing hundreds of thousands and millions of displaced people across the new borders of India and Pakistan.

column1: Why did the Muslim League break away from the Indian National Congress?
column2: Muslim fears of Hindu dominance after Congress' electoral victories led Muhammad Ali Jinnah to pursue a separate Muslim state, culminating in Pakistan.

column1: How did WWII affect Indian nationalist politics?
column2: The war effort mobilized India but alienated Muslims and increased communal tensions; the British exploited Hindu-Muslim rivalry to weaken the nationalist movement.

column1: When was Asian decolonization mostly complete?
column2: By 1950, except for French Indochina which continued to resist colonial rule.

column1: What major events occurred in the Middle East post-WWII?
column2: Nationalization of oil in Iran (1951), overthrow of Mussadiq in 1953, Free Officers' revolution in Egypt (1952), and nationalist movements in Iraq and Syria.

column1: How did the French respond to Algerian independence movements?
column2: With brutal warfare (1954-62), institutionalizing torture, causing political crises in France, until independence was granted under de Gaulle.

column1: What happened to Tunisia, Morocco, and Sudan during decolonization?
column2: Tunisia and Morocco gained independence peacefully in the 1950s; Sudan was quietly released by the British after losing control over Egypt.

column1: What was the Suez Crisis of 1956 and its outcome?
column2: A failed military operation by Britain, France, and Israel to overthrow Egypt's Nasser; it ended under U.S. pressure, pushing Egypt toward the USSR and ending Britain's Middle East dominance.

column1: Which colonial power resisted decolonization longest and why?
column2: Portugal resisted the longest because its weak, isolated economy depended on direct control of African resources.

column1: Why did South Africa and Southern Rhodesia resist decolonization?
column2: They had substantial white settler populations and sought to avoid African-dominated regimes; Southern Rhodesia even declared unilateral independence in 1965.

column1: What strategy did Britain, France, and Belgium prefer for decolonization?
column2: They preferred voluntary formal independence with continued economic and cultural dependence to avoid Left-wing regimes.

column1: What was the Mau Mau movement?
column2: A popular insurrection and guerrilla war (1952–56) in Kenya, mainly involving the Kikuyu people, against British colonial rule.

column1: What happened in the Belgian Congo after independence?
column2: The colony quickly descended into anarchy, civil war, and international power struggles.

column1: When did Ghana (Gold Coast) gain independence and who led it?
column2: In 1957, led by Kwame Nkrumah, a pan-African intellectual and politician.

column1: How did Guinea gain independence and what was its consequence?
column2: Guinea refused to join de Gaulle's French Community in 1958, gained early impoverished independence, and sought help from Moscow.

column1: When were most British, French, and Belgian African colonies decolonized?
column2: Between 1960 and 1962, with some later.

column1: When were the larger British Caribbean colonies decolonized?
column2: In the 1960s, with smaller islands following until 1981.

column1: By 1970, which territories remained under colonial or settler control?
column2: Only Central and Southern Africa (e.g., South Africa, Rhodesia) and Vietnam remained under direct control.

column1: How is the imperial era remembered after its end?
column2: As a sentimental part of literary and cinematic memories, while indigenous writers began producing post-independence literature.

column1: What did Frank Roberts say about the Soviet threat in 1946?
column2: He stated the Soviet menace was fundamentally different from the German threat, with less danger of sudden catastrophe.

column1: What does Richard Barnet describe about the Cold War economy?
column2: It supported millions working on nuclear weapons, scientists seeking breakthroughs, contractors profiting, and intellectuals promoting war and threats.

column1: How long did the Cold War last, according to the text?
column2: About forty-five years, from the dropping of the atomic bombs to the end of the Soviet Union.

column1: How did Thomas Hobbes’s definition of war apply to the Cold War?
column2: War includes prolonged periods where the will to fight is known, which fits the Cold War's long tense standoff without direct battles.

column1: Why was the Cold War considered a "Third World War"?
column2: Because it was a continuous global confrontation between superpowers without direct battle, dominated by the threat of nuclear war.

column1: Was there an imminent danger of world war during the Cold War?
column2: Objectively no, both superpowers accepted a balance of power and avoided direct military confrontation between them.

column1: What was the division of influence after WWII?
column2: USSR controlled Eastern Europe and communist zones; USA controlled the rest of the capitalist world, western hemisphere, and oceans.

column1: How was Germany divided after WWII?
column2: Into Eastern (Soviet) and Western (Allied) occupation zones; Austria became neutral.

column1: How did the USA handle post-war Japan?
column2: The USA unilaterally occupied Japan, excluding the USSR and other allies.

column1: What was the major zone of Cold War friction outside Europe?
column2: The post-colonial states in Asia, where the US and USSR competed for influence, often leading to conflict.

column1: How did the USSR view the Chinese communist takeover?
column2: The USSR did not want it but accepted it when it happened.

column1: What was the political alignment of most new post-colonial states?
column2: Mostly non-communist, anti-communist domestically, and non-aligned internationally.

column1: What does “mutually assured destruction” (MAD) mean?
column2: The idea that nuclear war would lead to total annihilation of both sides, preventing them from starting a nuclear conflict.

column1: How did the superpowers handle the Korean War regarding direct confrontation?
column2: USSR did not officially fight but covertly supported China, avoiding direct war with the USA.

column1: What was the significance of the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962)?
column2: It was the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war, highlighting the dangers of misinterpretation and escalating tensions.

column1: What was the "Cold Peace" concept?
column2: The tacit understanding that despite rhetoric, the superpowers would avoid direct war and try peaceful coexistence.

column1: How did the West react to the 1956 Hungarian Revolution?
column2: The West did not intervene, acknowledging Soviet control over Eastern Europe.

column1: What role did espionage and secret services play in the Cold War?
column2: They conducted covert operations and inspired spy fiction, but had limited real power impact except in weaker Third World countries.

column1: When did the US President dismiss General MacArthur, and why?
column2: In 1951, for pushing military ambition too far during the Korean War.

column1: What nuclear weapons milestones occurred between 1949 and 1953?
column2: USSR developed the atom bomb (1949) and hydrogen bomb (1953), matching US nuclear capability.

column1: How was nuclear threat used politically during the Cold War?
column2: Both sides used nuclear threat for negotiation and domestic politics without intending actual use.

column1: What was the Western belief about global stability after WWII that fueled the Cold War?
column2: That the world was unstable and a new world war might break out at any moment, held back only by mutual deterrence.

column1: What economic concern overshadowed war prevention in the US post-WWII plans?
column2: Preventing another Great Depression or severe post-war economic crisis.

column1: Why were Americans worried about post-war Europe and Asia?
column2: They feared desperate and radicalized peoples might support social revolution and policies incompatible with free enterprise.

column1: How strong were communist parties in post-war Europe?
column2: They emerged stronger than ever, sometimes as the largest electoral forces in their countries.

column1: What role did economic hardship in 1946–47 play in Cold War fears?
column2: Poor harvests and harsh winters increased political and economic instability, making Western leaders more nervous.

column1: Why did the wartime alliance between capitalist West and socialist USSR break down?
column2: Because of inherent ideological differences and post-war political instability.

column1: Why was US public rhetoric about a Soviet global conquest exaggerated?
column2: It reflected political campaign rhetoric and Cold War fears more than actual Soviet intentions.

column1: How did some British diplomats describe the Cold War threat?
column2: As a modern religious war between Soviet communism and Western capitalism/social democracy.

column1: Did the USSR plan to expand communism aggressively after WWII?
column2: No, it was defensive and limited to agreed spheres of influence from 1943–45 summits.

column1: What kind of communist regimes did Moscow promote in client states?
column2: Mixed economies with multi-party parliamentary democracies, not single-party dictatorships.

column1: Which communist state refused to follow Moscow’s model?
column2: Yugoslavia.

column1: How did the USSR’s military strength change after WWII?
column2: It was rapidly demobilized from 12 million troops in 1945 to 3 million by 1948.

column1: What was the actual state of the USSR after WWII?
column2: Ruined economy, exhausted population, fragile regime, ruled by risk-averse Stalin.

column1: What was Stalin’s approach to relations with the USA post-war?
column2: Defensive and cautious, avoiding antagonizing the only power capable of helping economically.

column1: Did Soviet planners think capitalism was in crisis after WWII?
column2: No, they believed capitalism under US hegemony would continue for a long time.

column1: What was the main cause of Cold War confrontation according to the text?
column2: Mutual insecurity and precarious positions of USSR and USA, not ideology alone.

column1: Who formulated the US "containment" policy and what was his view on Soviet communism?
column2: George Kennan; he saw Soviet Russia as insecure and power-focused, not an ideological crusader.

column1: How did Moscow view negotiation over post-war territorial arrangements?
column2: As unacceptable to reopen Yalta agreements; Moscow was inflexible.

column1: Why did the USA not give economic aid to the USSR after WWII?
column2: They claimed to have lost the Soviet loan request and gave nothing without concessions.

column1: What did Moscow fear most after WWII?
column2: The immediate global hegemony of the USA and being turned into a US client economy.

column1: Does mutual intransigence between superpowers imply a daily danger of war?
column2: No, moments of open confrontation were rare and war crises even rarer despite power rivalry.

column1: Which was more dangerous, the USSR or the USA’s political system, according to the text?
column2: The USA’s democracy was probably more dangerous due to the need to win votes and public opinion.

column1: Why did American politicians use apocalyptic anti-communism rhetoric?
column2: It helped win elections, raise funds for policy, and justify continued US global leadership.

column1: Who was James Forrestal and what happened to him?
column2: Truman’s Secretary of State for the Navy, who suffered mental illness and committed suicide fearing a Soviet attack.

column1: Why was anti-communism popular in the USA?
column2: Because American identity (“Americanism”) was ideologically opposite to communism and supported individualism and private enterprise.

column1: Who initiated the anti-Red witch-hunt in the USA?
column2: Demagogues like Senator Joseph McCarthy and FBI chief J. Edgar Hoover exploited anti-communism for political gain.

column1: What conflicting demands did US politicians face regarding Cold War policy?
column2: To roll back communism, save money, and interfere as little as possible with Americans' comfort.

column1: What nuclear strategy did the US announce in 1954?
column2: “Massive retaliation”: threatening nuclear weapons even in response to limited conventional attacks.

column1: What was the consequence of the US nuclear strategy?
column2: The US committed to an aggressive stance with minimal tactical flexibility.

column1: What term did President Eisenhower use for the war-preparation industry?
column2: The “military-industrial complex.”

column1: How did the military-industrial complexes of the superpowers use their excess capacity?
column2: By arming allies and clients and selling weapons, while keeping top arms for themselves.

column1: Which countries acquired nuclear weapons besides the USA and USSR during the Cold War?
column2: Britain (1952), France, China (1960s), and later Israel, South Africa, and probably India.

column1: When did nuclear proliferation become a serious international problem?
column2: After the end of the bipolar superpower order in 1989.

column1: What is a balanced historical view on responsibility for the Cold War?
column2: Mutual fear and confrontation escalated tensions; blame cannot be assigned solely to the USSR or USA.

column1: What events illustrate the Cold War’s “congealing” of fronts?
column2: Partition of Germany, building of the Berlin Wall, Western alliance under US leadership, Eastern bloc under Moscow.

column1: Did Western European governments support American anti-communism?
column2: Yes, all were wholeheartedly anti-communist and preferred US protection over trusting the USSR.

column1: Was communism a significant domestic political threat in the USA?
column2: No, it was politically insignificant, unlike the strong anti-communism used in elections.

column1: What was the real issue behind American Cold War rhetoric?
column2: Maintaining real US global supremacy rather than an actual threat of communist world domination.

column1: Were NATO allies happy with US policy?
column2: They were uneasy but accepted US supremacy as necessary protection against the USSR.

column1: What was the common policy toward communism among Western democracies?
column2: Containment of communism, not its immediate destruction.

column1: What was the most obvious face of the Cold War?
column2: Military confrontation and a frenetic nuclear arms race in the West.

column1: Did the nuclear powers ever use their nuclear weapons during the Cold War?
column2: No, nuclear weapons were never used.

column1: In which three major wars did nuclear powers engage during the Cold War?
column2: Korean War, Vietnam War, and the Soviet-Afghan War.

column1: What was the outcome of the Korean War intervention by the US and allies?
column2: The result was a draw, with no clear victory.

column1: What was the outcome of the US intervention in Vietnam?
column2: The US lost the war.

column1: How long did the USSR support the Afghan government before withdrawing?
column2: About eight years, withdrawing in 1988.

column1: What did the Cold War's constant threat of war produce internationally?
column2: Peace movements, especially against nuclear arms, and anti-war movements like the American youth movement against the Vietnam War.

column1: What was the political impact of the Cold War on European governments post-1947?
column2: Split into pro-communist and anti-communist regimes, with communists excluded from Western governments.

column1: How did the USSR consolidate power in Eastern Europe after the war?
column2: Eliminated non-communists from ‘people’s democracies’ and established ‘dictatorships of the proletariat’.

column1: What was the Cominform and what happened to it?
column2: The Communist Information Bureau, set up to confront the USA; dissolved in 1956 when tensions cooled.

column1: Why did Stalin not install a satellite government in Finland?
column2: Possibly to avoid risking a war, as Finns had previously fought the USSR.

column1: Which Eastern European leader broke with Moscow in 1948?
column2: Marshal Tito of Yugoslavia.

column1: How did the US influence political systems in post-war Japan and Italy?
column2: Encouraged single-party dominance: Liberal-Democratic Party in Japan and Christian Democrats in Italy.

column1: What was the political consequence of US policy in Japan and Italy?
column2: Stabilization of communists/socialists as opposition and institutionalized government corruption.

column1: What political spectrum was excluded from Western Cold War governments?
column2: National Socialism, fascism, overt Japanese nationalism, and much of the right-wing nationalist sector.

column1: Which parties played a central role in Western Europe’s Cold War politics?
column2: Christian-Democratic parties linked to the Catholic Church, with solid anti-fascist records.

column1: What new political organization emerged in Europe due to the Cold War?
column2: The European Community (later the European Union), aiming for economic and some political integration.

column1: What fears did the European Community reflect besides fear of the USSR?
column2: Fear of a revived Germany and suspicion of US interests overriding European allies.

column1: What was the Marshall Plan and its purpose?
column2: A massive US program (launched in 1947) to aid European economic recovery and prevent Soviet expansion.

column1: Did the US successfully impose its ideal post-war economic system on Europe?
column2: No, it proved unrealistic due to Europe’s economic difficulties and resistance from Britain and France.

column1: What was the European Coal and Steel Community?
column2: A French proposal for European economic integration, the forerunner of the European Community.

column1: Who dominated NATO and its military plans?
column2: The United States.

column1: What happened to the Anglo-French Suez war of 1956?
column2: It was aborted under American pressure, showing US dominance.

column1: How did the economic balance shift during the Cold War?
column2: From US dominance to growing European and Japanese economic power.

column1: What caused the weakening of the US dollar during the Cold War?
column2: Large deficits from military spending, especially the Vietnam War, and social welfare programs.

column1: What was the ‘Gold Pool’ and what happened to it?
column2: A cooperative effort by central banks to stabilize gold prices; it dissolved in 1968 due to drained reserves.

column1: When was the US dollar’s convertibility to gold formally abandoned?
column2: August 1971.

column1: What was the impact of the end of the dollar-gold convertibility?
column2: Ended the international payment system’s stability and US control over it.

column1: How was the 1991 Gulf War financed?
column2: Mainly by other countries supporting the US, showing US economic limits.

column1: What was unusual about the 1991 Gulf War for a major power?
column2: It was one of the rare wars from which a major power made a profit.

column1: When did the Cold War appear to take tentative steps towards détente?
column2: In the early 1960s.

column1: Who was N.S. Khrushchev and what was his role in the Cold War?
column2: Soviet leader after Stalin who believed in reform and peaceful coexistence, and led from 1958–64.

column1: What major crises did détente have to survive in the early 1960s?
column2: Confrontations between Khrushchev's impulsive decisions and JFK's gesture politics.

column1: What major communist success shocked the West in the early 1960s?
column2: The communist victory in Cuba.

column1: What strained the USSR's relations during Khrushchev's leadership?
column2: The rupture with China, which accused Moscow of softening on capitalism.

column1: What symbolized the tacit agreement between the superpowers not to frighten each other?
column2: The installation of the ‘hot line’ between the White House and the Kremlin in 1963.

column1: What significant barrier was built in 1961 between East and West Europe?
column2: The Berlin Wall.

column1: What nuclear arms control efforts took place during the 1960s and early 1970s?
column2: Test-ban treaties, attempts to stop nuclear proliferation, SALT agreements, and Anti-Ballistic Missile (ABM) treaties.

column1: How did trade relations between the USA and USSR change in the late 1960s?
column2: Trade began to flourish after being politically strangled for years.

column1: What marked the beginning of the "Second Cold War"?
column2: The mid-1970s, coinciding with the global economic crisis starting in 1973.

column1: How did the 1973 oil crisis affect the global economic and political landscape?
column2: OPEC's oil price rise suggested a weakening of US international domination.

column1: What war demoralized and divided the USA during the Cold War?
column2: The Vietnam War (1965–75).

column1: How did America's European allies respond to the Vietnam War?
column2: They did not send troops to fight alongside the USA.

column1: What conflict in 1973 further revealed US isolation from its allies?
column2: The Yom Kippur War between Israel and Soviet-backed Egypt and Syria.

column1: How did European allies react to US requests for airbase use during the Yom Kippur War?
column2: Except for Portugal, they refused to allow US planes to use bases on their soil.

column1: What action did US Secretary of State Henry Kissinger take during the Yom Kippur War?
column2: Declared the first nuclear alert since the Cuban Missile Crisis.

column1: How did OPEC influence US allies during the 1973 Middle East conflict?
column2: By cutting oil supplies and threatening embargoes, prioritizing their own oil interests over support for Israel.

column1: What was the effect of the Third World revolutions between 1974 and 1979?
column2: Many new regimes aligned with the USSR, gaining it military and naval bases outside its borders.

column1: How did the USA shift its Cold War strategy after Vietnam?
column2: Avoided committing troops directly, instead engaging indirectly in Third World conflicts.

column1: What was a major achievement of US diplomacy under Nixon and Kissinger during détente?
column2: Expulsion of Soviets from Egypt and recruiting China into an anti-Soviet alliance.

column1: Which Portuguese colonies fell under communist rule during the 1970s?
column2: Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Cape Verde.

column1: What happened to the Ethiopian emperor during this period?
column2: He was overthrown by a communist-aligned revolution.

column1: What was the significance of Soviet naval expansion in the 1970s?
column2: It acquired major new bases on either side of the Indian Ocean, signaling global reach.

column1: How did the USSR’s entry into Afghanistan influence American perceptions?
column2: It was seen (incorrectly) as the first step toward Soviet control of the Indian Ocean and Persian Gulf.

column1: How did Soviet defense spending change after 1964?
column2: Increased by 4–5% annually for twenty years, contributing to economic strain.

column1: By 1976, how did Soviet missile launcher numbers compare to the US?
column2: The USSR had 25% more missile launchers than the US.

column1: What was the strategic value of the Soviet navy's global presence?
column2: Mostly political symbolism of superpower status, less strategic sense.

column1: Why did American Cold Warriors see Soviet naval ambitions as a threat?
column2: It signaled Soviet refusal to remain regionally confined, threatening Western supremacy.

column1: Was US power realistically weaker than Soviet power in the early 1980s?
column2: No, US power was decisively greater than Soviet power despite hysteria in Washington.

column1: How did Soviet economic production in the 1980s compare to the US?
column2: The USSR produced more steel, pig-iron, and tractors but lagged behind in technology like silicon and software.

column1: Did the USSR plan a military attack on the West in the 1980s?
column2: No, there was no evidence or likelihood that the USSR wanted war against the West.

column1: What effect did Western military alarmism in the early 1980s have?
column2: It convinced Soviets of a possible pre-emptive Western nuclear attack and sparked large European anti-nuclear movements.

column1: How do historians view US government behavior during early Reagan years?
column2: As irrational, driven by trauma from 1970s defeats, scandals, and crises.

column1: What events contributed to US political trauma before Reagan?
column2: Nixon’s resignation, weak successors, Iran hostage crisis, Central American revolutions, and the 2nd oil crisis.

column1: What was Reagan's main motive behind his military actions like Grenada and Libya?
column2: To restore US prestige and demonstrate unchallengeable military supremacy.

column1: How did the Gulf War of 1991 relate to earlier US humiliations?
column2: It was a belated compensation for earlier moments when the US could not respond to Third World threats to its oil.

column1: What was the real purpose of Reagan's "Evil Empire" crusade?
column2: Therapy for the US rather than a practical plan to re-establish world power balance.

column1: How had NATO and US allies acted before Reagan's presidency?
column2: NATO rearmed quietly in the late 1970s, with support from European social-democratic governments.

column1: What role did Cuba play in Soviet Cold War strategy in Africa?
column2: Cuba sent expeditionary forces supporting Soviet-aligned movements in Africa.

column1: What ideological shift occurred in the West around 1980?
column2: Rise of right-wing governments advocating extreme laissez-faire capitalism, e.g., Reagan and Thatcher.

column1: What did Reagan’s Cold War rhetoric target besides communism?
column2: The liberal welfare state and Rooseveltian policies at home.

column1: Was the US government prepared for the USSR’s collapse?
column2: No, it was surprised and did not expect or plan for the Soviet collapse.

column1: How did Reagan personally view US-Soviet relations?
column2: He believed in coexistence but wanted a world free of nuclear weapons.

column1: Who played the crucial role in convincing the West of a genuine Soviet desire to end the Cold War?
column2: Mikhail Gorbachev.

column1: Why was it easier for a Soviet leader to initiate ending the arms race?
column2: The USSR did not have to manage crusading public opinion like the US.

column1: Why was it harder for a Soviet leader to convince the West of their sincerity?
column2: Due to the lack of public opinion pressure and suspicion from the West.

column1: What summits are considered to have practically ended the Cold War?
column2: The Reykjavik Summit (1986) and Washington Summit (1987).

column1: Are the end of the Cold War and the end of the Soviet system the same phenomenon?
column2: No, they are historically separable but connected phenomena.

column1: What did Soviet socialism claim to be in relation to capitalism?
column2: A global alternative to the capitalist world system.

column1: Why was socialism falling behind capitalism after 1960?
column2: It was no longer competitive economically or technologically.

column1: How did the arms race affect the US and the USSR economies differently?
column2: The US could absorb military debt due to a larger economy; the USSR was strained because military spending was a larger proportion of its economy.

column1: How did the economic size of the European Community and Japan compare to the US by the late 1970s?
column2: Together, they were 60% larger than the US economy.

column1: Did Soviet allies and dependents contribute economically to the USSR?
column2: No, they were a constant drain on Soviet resources.

column1: What proportion of the world did backward countries represent demographically and economically?
column2: They were 80% of the world demographically but peripheral economically.

column1: What was the main cause of socialism’s vulnerability according to the text?
column2: Its economic defects combined with invasion by the dynamic capitalist economy.

column1: How did socialist leaders respond to the global economy in the 1970s?
column2: They exploited resources like oil prices and loans instead of reforming their economic systems, which worsened their problems.

column1: What paradox about détente and the Cold War does the text highlight?
column2: That détente, meant to ease tensions, actually helped lead to the Soviet system's downfall.

column1: When did the "real" Cold War end according to the text?
column2: At the Washington summit of 1987.

column1: What event made it impossible to deny that the Cold War had ended?
column2: The collapse of the Soviet Empire in 1989 and the dissolution of the USSR (1989-91).

column1: How did the Cold War affect global rivalries before WWII?
column2: It eliminated or overshadowed almost all rivalries except between the two superpowers.

column1: Why did France and West Germany bury their historical conflict after 1947?
column2: Because of their common membership in the US-led camp and US hegemony in Western Europe.

column1: How did NATO influence Germany and Japan’s status post-WWII?
column2: They were subordinate members of the US alliance, limiting fears about their military power.

column1: What was the USSR’s real fear regarding Germany during the Cold War?
column2: NATO missiles on German soil, not the German armed forces themselves.

column1: Did the Cold War era see a reduction in armed conflicts globally?
column2: No, except in Europe conflicts continued but were controlled to avoid nuclear war.

column1: Why didn’t Iraq’s claims against Kuwait lead to war before 1989?
column2: Because the Cold War rivalry acted as a deterrent, with the USSR discouraging Iraqi adventurism.

column1: How did superpowers treat political changes in allied states during the Cold War?
column2: They intervened if changes threatened allegiance, e.g., USSR troops in Hungary and Czechoslovakia.

column1: Which countries had broken away from Soviet control before 1970?
column2: Yugoslavia, Albania, and China.

column1: Did Soviet-allied Third World countries tolerate local communist parties?
column2: Hardly any did.

column1: What kept the world’s divisions more or less stable during the Cold War?
column2: Power, political influence, bribery, bipolarity, and anti-imperialism logic.

column1: Could superpowers control or prevent home-grown revolutions in allied states?
column2: No, such revolutions were beyond their direct control.

column1: Did NATO allies withdraw despite issues with US alignment?
column2: No, even troubled allies like Germany stayed in the alliance.

column1: Why did unstable and weak regimes survive during the Cold War?
column2: The threat of nuclear war (the “mushroom cloud”) deterred major conflicts.

column1: What was a Cold War benefit for small states?
column2: It was the best time to survive as mini-states due to superpower protection.

column1: What changed about the international system after the Cold War?
column2: The freezing and stabilization of rivalries ended, allowing old and new conflicts to re-emerge.

column1: How did the Cold War affect global arms proliferation?
column2: It filled the world with arms to an unbelievable degree due to constant superpower competition and low-intensity wars.

column1: What role did military-industrial complexes play during the Cold War?
column2: They had an economic interest in exporting arms abroad to justify their large military budgets.

column1: Who were the main buyers of arms during the Cold War?
column2: Military governments worldwide, supported by superpower aid and local revenues, especially from oil-rich states.

column1: Why did socialist economies and declining capitalist states export arms?
column2: They had few competitive products and relied on arms exports as a major source of income.

column1: Name three weapons that became widely known in the late twentieth century’s guerrilla and urban conflicts.
column2: Uzi machine-gun (Israeli), Kalashnikov rifle (Russian), and Semtex explosive (Czech).

column1: How did the Cold War perpetuate itself after its official end?
column2: Through ongoing local conflicts fueled by superpower-supported clients and continued arms supplies.

column1: What happened in Angola after the Cold War officially ended?
column2: UNITA rebels continued fighting despite withdrawal of South African and Cuban forces and disavowal by the US and UN.

column1: What is an example of a Cold War-era weapon that became a problem post-war due to proliferation?
column2: The US-supplied Stinger anti-aircraft missiles in Afghanistan, which were resold on the arms market.

column1: What famous phrase summarizes the problem of uncontrollable forces unleashed by Cold War arms distribution?
column2: Goethe’s "Die ich rief die Geister, werd’ ich nun nicht los." ("The spirits I summoned, I cannot get rid of.")

column1: What immediate effect did the end of the Cold War have on international and domestic political structures?
column2: It removed the props that had supported them, leaving much of the world in disarray and partial collapse.

column1: Why was the idea of a ‘new world order’ after the Cold War unrealistic?
column2: Because the bi-polar order couldn’t simply be replaced by one superpower due to deep systemic changes and loss of old landmarks.

column1: How did wealthy capitalist economies react to the collapse of the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe?
column2: They reacted sluggishly and underestimated the political and economic consequences.

column1: Which country particularly misunderstood the challenges of reunifying after the Cold War?
column2: West Germany, especially in dealing with the former German Democratic Republic.

column1: How did the end of the Cold War coincide with global economic conditions?
column2: It coincided with a major crisis in capitalism’s world economy and the final crisis of the Soviet system.

column1: What is the historical significance of the years around 1990?
column2: They mark a secular turning point signaling the end of an era, not just for the East but the whole world.

column1: What remains uncertain after the Cold War ended?
column2: The nature and prospects of the new world order that would replace the old Cold War system.

column1: What fundamental changes since the Cold War began should have more emphasis in future history books?
column2: The extraordinary, unprecedented transformations of the world economy and human societies.

column1: When did Modena experience its great leap forward in living standards?
column2: In the past forty years from Italian Unification, with transformation accelerating rapidly.

column1: According to J.K. Galbraith, what influences consumer demand once basic needs are met?
column2: Management influences consumer demand when people are well-fed, well-clad, and well-sheltered.

column1: What slogan did the British Conservative premier use to win the 1959 election?
column2: "You’ve never had it so good."

column1: When did economists begin to recognize the post-WWII boom as an exceptional phase?
column2: Mainly after the boom ended in the 1970s and 1980s.

column1: What names describe the post-WWII economic boom period?
column2: The French called it "les trente glorieuses" (the thirty glorious years); Anglo-Americans called it the "Golden Age."

column1: Why was the Golden Age less revolutionary for the USA?
column2: The US economy had already expanded during WWII and suffered no damage, so growth continued rather than surged.

column1: Between 1950 and 1973, how did US growth compare with other industrial countries?
column2: The US grew more slowly than any other industrial country except Britain and was no more dynamic than earlier periods.

column1: How did GDP per capita in the US compare to France, Germany, Japan, and Britain in 1950?
column2: US GDP per capita was double France and Germany, five times Japan's, and 50% higher than Britain's.

column1: When did the material benefits of growth become generally felt in Britain and Italy?
column2: Mid-1950s in Britain; in the 1960s in Italy’s Emilia-Romagna.

column1: When did full employment become common in Western Europe during the Golden Age?
column2: In the 1960s, with unemployment averaging 1.5%.

column1: What percentage of world production and manufacturing exports did developed capitalist countries represent during the Golden Age?
column2: About 75% of production and over 80% of manufacturing exports.

column1: How did the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe perform economically in the 1950s?
column2: The USSR grew faster than any Western country in the 1950s; Eastern Europe also grew rapidly but slowed in the 1960s.

column1: Did communist East Germany grow faster than non-communist West Germany during the Golden Age?
column2: No, East Germany lagged behind West Germany.

column1: Was the Golden Age’s prosperity global?
column2: It was worldwide but affluence did not reach the majority of the world's population, especially in poorer countries.

column1: How did global population change during the Golden Age?
column2: The populations of Africa, East Asia, South Asia, and Latin America more than doubled between 1950 and 1985.

column1: Did mass starvation occur during the Golden Age?
column2: No mass starvation except due to wars and political disasters, such as in China.

column1: How much did life expectancy increase between the late 1930s and late 1960s?
column2: By an average of seven years, and up to seventeen years comparing those periods.

column1: How did food production per capita change in developing regions during the 1950s and 1960s?
column2: Food production per capita rose in all regions except Latin America in the 1950s; Latin America also grew slightly; in the 1960s growth slowed but was positive.

column1: How did food production per capita change in the 1970s and 1980s in poorer regions?
column2: Some regions (Far East, Latin America) outpaced population growth; others (Africa, Central America, Asian Near East) fell behind or declined.

column1: What problem did the developed world face regarding food production in the 1980s?
column2: It produced so much surplus food that it either grew less or dumped it below cost, harming poor country producers.

column1: What are "butter mountains" and "milk lakes"?
column2: Terms used to describe massive surpluses of dairy products in the European Community.

column1: How did food surpluses in rich countries affect producers in poor countries?
column2: They undercut poor country producers by selling at lower prices, sometimes cheaper than in the producing country itself.

column1: Which regions experienced dramatic industrial revolutions during the Golden Age?
column2: Spain and Finland in the capitalist West; Bulgaria and Romania in socialist agrarian countries.

column1: What happened to the number of countries dependent mainly on agriculture for imports by the late 1980s?
column2: It diminished sharply, with only about fifteen states paying for half or more of their imports from farm exports.

column1: How much did world output of manufactured goods grow between the early 1950s and early 1970s?
column2: It quadrupled.

column1: How much did world trade in manufactured products grow during the same period?
column2: It grew tenfold.

column1: How did world agricultural output increase during the Golden Age?
column2: Mainly by raising productivity, not by expanding cultivated land.

column1: How much did grain yields per hectare increase between 1950–52 and 1980–82?
column2: They almost doubled globally and more than doubled in North America, Western Europe, and East Asia.

column1: What environmental issue emerged as a by-product of the Golden Age’s industrial growth?
column2: Pollution and ecological deterioration.
